speaker: interviewer
text: It's April 15, 1996. And this is tape number two. All right, Leon, you were telling me that the-- the entire operation was moved. And tell me what happened at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, we stayed on a blind alley on the main railroad tracks, on a blind alley waiting for knowledge where to go. But all transport shall go next because the place where we supposed to go was taken by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was taken by the Germans. So we didn't. Then they received an order to go to a place, Vereshchagino. And it happened that in the Soviet Union, they had two places by the same name. One was near Khabarovsk, which is the end of the Soviet Union. And the other one was in the Urals.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of going to the right place, we went to the wrong place. And it took us almost six months until we came to the right place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions on the way were terrible. We had no food, no water to drink. We used to go down while we were standing on the blind railroad tracks because the main railroad-- there is only one line which goes from Siberia to-- to the end of-- to the Chinese border, one main track. If the military were supposed to come through, they pushed us on the side. And we stayed there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a single track?

speaker: interviewee
text: A single track. So we stayed there. So we used to drink the water from the side roads, put away the worms and sip a little bit of water. And this was in [? Auschman. ?] The weather was bad so we were sleeping. I was lucky with my partner. We had a cabin from one of the trucks, which were on the open railroad track. So we used to sleep there, and go all the way there. When it was nice, we used to sleep underneath it because they weren't yet finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but when we came to Vereshchagino, finally, I went back. Vereshchagino was also-- by 13 miles from the railroad. And there was a sideline railroad. And I went back to work on the railroad until in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- by the end of '41. In '42, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took me to a labor camp to work for the army. I was there-- this was under military rules already, a labor camp for the military. Coming there, I seen that it's no picnic. So I demanded to go to a medical commission.

speaker: interviewer
text: You demanded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I demanded. I asked for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while, I was put there in military commission. I was born-- I was born legally blind. And when I stayed to the preserve for the military in Poland, I was free. Even in the war time, they will not take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was pretty sure that the laws govern everywhere the same. And I was right. They freed me as an invalid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1942, by August, I came to my uncle-- back. My uncle was evacuated from Moscow to Ufa. This was on the Urals. And I went there. I stayed with him a few-- two weeks, three weeks. Then I decided to go to Central Asia, closer to Israel. And I made my way to Tashkent. From Tashkent, I went to Samarkand. Then I went to join the Polish army in Kermine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there I remained. Of course, on the day when we were supposed to get out of the Soviet Union, came a group of Poles-- the last minute. And they took out about 12 Jewish boys. And they put all the Poles in. They went out to Tehran. And we remained in the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, a Jewish Polkovnik, a high military official, who used to be the connector between the Soviet Union and the Polish army, which was created in the Soviet Union due to an agreement with the-- the Polish general. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And came over and told us, look-- and he said it in Jewish-- maybe it's better for you that they are going to have a possibility of not coming back. And if you'll stay here, believe me, the Germans will not come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town were you in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Kermine.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kermine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kermine. And I remained in Kermine there. As an invalid, I joined the [? Altel, ?] a group of-- [? Altel ?] is a group of entertainment-- like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Singers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Entertainment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not entertainment, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Enterprise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Enterprises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, enterprises.

speaker: interviewee
text: Enterprises, excuse me-- tailors, shoemakers, restaurant, barbers, carpenters--

speaker: interviewer
text: Services.

speaker: interviewee
text: This type-- services. I joined them. I worked myself up to the position of the leader-- the technical leader of the [? Altel. ?] And I worked until one day, I was approached in a way that I couldn't resist to go become the administrator of the Polish Children's Home. After the departure of the Poles from-- from the Soviet Union, there were still-- they had Polish children, mostly Jewish whose parents perished-- they died of all kind of diseases which were there at that time-- and Polish children, Ukrainian children, which belonged to Poland before. And the Soviet Union created a number of these Polish children's homes. And from that day, we had a supervisor, which was a Jew, a communist from Leningrad, by the name of Mr. Berizanski. I worked as administrator. They had a lady from Krakow by the name of Miss Singer. She used to be the supervisor over the education-- the Polish education. And my wife works there-- worked there as a teacher. And this is the way we lived in back there together until 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there, yeah. In 1945, October 12th, I married my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: After how many years of knowing her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just since 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years. And we worked for [? them. ?] But at that time, they had an order to collect the children. All Ukrainian children whose family were living in the liberated part of Ukraine-- which belongs now to Ukraine, not to Poland-- to bring them back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So between me and my supervisor, we decided I'd bring the children back to Lvov. And I went there. And from Lvov, I went to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Poland by-- in November, I was in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of '45. I was in [INAUDIBLE]. This was in the-- in the new territory which was freed from the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Silesia. And I was there. And I stayed in contact with my wife. And when they approached coming back to Poland with the children, I joined them. And we wind up in Gostynin, near Kutno. And over there, we join them. Coming to Gostynin, seeing the children and my wife and all the friends which I worked with. We came a problem knowing what's going to be with the Jewish children. They intended to send all the Jewish children away in-- in certain homes in Poland, together with the Christians and everything, which I didn't like. So being there before they were coming-- so I got already connected with the Bricha. This was a group of young Jewish people whose goal was to save Jewish souls-- souls. And one night, all Jewish children disappeared. They went to the Hashomer Hatzair kibbutz in Lodz. And they now, thank god, are all in Israel. I don't know. I'm not in contact with them. I was in Israel. I didn't see them. They didn't come out this way. I didn't know where they are. And I don't drive a car. And I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: They probably went to all various places.

speaker: interviewee
text: All various places.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were responsible for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am. I take responsibility for taking these Jewish childrens out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I settled Lodz. In Lodz, I joined the Hashomer Hatzair back. I became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a leader in high position. And I was close there to take a kibbutz out. After the Kielce pogrom, I was given a kibbutz, which called [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you just mentioned the Kielce Pogrom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the Kielce pogrom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Lodz. But at that time was a pogrom in Kielce where 42 Jews died at the hand of the Polish Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war-- this was-- this was in 19-- this was in-- in June or May--

speaker: interviewer
text: In May, the war ended. So '45, June of '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was in '46. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '46. This was in '46--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Kielce pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with the Kielce pogrom? They-- they murdered--

speaker: interviewee
text: They murdered 42 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: 42 Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce, Poland. And we, as part of the protection of Jews in Lodz, we used-- we had an organized brigade. We used to go out every night to keep patrol, to face the music. And there were many times which were good and danger. But thank god, everything went through. It didn't happen in the Lodz. And afterwards, after the Kielce Pogrom, we decided to close out, as-- leave as much we can, get out of Poland. So we started up. I became the [NON-ENGLISH] from the kibbutz. And I came with the kibbutz to Vienna. First, we went through the-- the borders within Poland and Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in-- near Prague, but in a small place, by two months. And the third month, we started up went to Wladyslawowo. And then Vienna-- we stayed in the [INAUDIBLE] house in Vienna. And from Vienna, we wind up in Salzburg. In Salzburg, we stayed three months. And one day, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in Salzburg, during the lunch hour, a young man comes over. We used to be in a barrack, a military barrack. It was a long one. And [INAUDIBLE] used to always look after things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was lunchtime. I had about 260 people in the hall. I see a young man coming over to the door. Over there was a [NON-ENGLISH] standing at guard. And he started to explain it to him. And I seen hands like this and that. So I decided to go over to see what's going on. As I-- as I was approaching, the young man started to shiver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come close, he embraces me, kisses me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look at him. After that, I asked him, look, I see you know who I am. Can you tell me who you are? But the way that my sentence fell out, he started to cry, his tears over his face. [SOBBING] I'm your youngest brother. He's my youngest brother. I didn't recognize him.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that I left-- Oh my god.

speaker: interviewee
text: --until now, he grew up. He became heavier, better looking. He became a man. And I couldn't recognize him. And whoever was there was crying. And believe me, there were people who went through the war in the woods, in the [? Wald, ?] like they say, partisans. And he told me that two other brothers are alive. They live in Stuttgart. I didn't know. And it changed my life a little bit. He went back home. And I told them that I'm coming. I had to attend to the business. I had to call in the-- our leadership. I had to give over the kibbutz to somebody else. And at that time-- day, when he came, the night before, I sent four people away to the border between Italy and Austria for us to go to Italy. So the-- the whole thing changed for me because I was willing to go to see my brothers. And I gave over the kibbutz. And this was the kibbutz who used to be responsible for the travel and exodus. This kibbutz was taken over by a different Arieh. My Hebrew name was also Arieh. A different Arieh, who was the only one person who the English people brought down on a stretcher from the ship. And the [? only ?] woman who died on that ship was my kibbutz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I came to Stuttgart. Next few days I've been in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, did your brother tell you anything about your parents of what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, the whole family, were killed in Treblinka-- my sisters, my uncles. My father had five brothers. The oldest brother, [NON-ENGLISH], was shot in his bed because he was a diabetic, sick. And he couldn't go to the march, so they shot him there in bed. He had a wife. He had three daughters and a son. One daughter has six children. Two daughters survived. One died in Tel Aviv. One lives in Haifa. The other one has four children. Two children survived. One lives in Germany. And one daughter lives in Rochester, New York. And the son with a wife, who used to be the granddaughter of the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi, with two kids, they went to Treblinka. And a daughter with a husband and a child, they went to Treblinka. Second brother had five kids. Four sons and a daughter. One son survived, lives in Berlin. The rest went to Treblinka. Another uncle had six kid-- children. Him and his-- he was killed in Schlieben, concentration camp Schlieben. His five kids want to Treblinka. My father had an older sister, [? Rochele. ?] She had a husband, three sons, and two daughters, all married with children. One daughter left Poland before the war. She lives in Paris. And one son survived, hidden all the time by a Pole, who didn't know them, and he didn't know them. They-- he survived there and married the daughter. And he lives in Red Bank, New Jersey, now. And another aunt, my father's sister, lived in Kozienice, Kuzma--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kozienice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kozienice. Had a husband and four kids, all vanished. And the uncle must have passed away last year at the age of 87.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are brought up to date, all of your family, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is from my father's side. My mother had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fraidle, a sister, an older sister, a husband. She had three sons and a daughter. The oldest son and the youngest son was murdered during the war. And the daughter had three kids with the husband. They all vanished. One son, he had a wife and two kids from Kielce. They vanished. He survived, and he died in-- in '89 in New York. Another sister, she lived in Lodz. She had six children. Two sons survived, but one was killed in Lodz after the war. And one died in Israel, Tel Aviv. And the rest, one daughter left Poland before the war to Argentina. She's alive with her kids there. On that end, my mother's younger sister, which photo of their wedding is here. She had eight children. None survived. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I have one aunt, who went in 1938, in December of 1938, to Argentina to Buenos Aires. The uncle and aunt died. Two cousins died there already. And I still have a cousin and a sister there, two sisters still living. This is more or less the first immediate family. But my grandfather's family and my-- my father's families, aunts and uncles, the others from the other part. We were a family-- the greatest family in the city of Zwolen, close to 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a handful survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a handful survived. That's all what I can tell you about family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met your youngest brother. And you were reunited with your other brothers in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. The-- the-- the reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Stuttgart, my brother had to leave because he was scheduled already to go to the United States. He had here one aunt with two children. She emigrated in 1923 to the United States. And she was living here in the Bronx. And she demanded to be the patron of us. Her older daughter was well-off. And they sent certificates for us. And we came to United States, and we settled down in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- your three brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: All four of us were in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, from the Bronx, there was a time when the farming was specially available for the newcomers to get on their own. They bought farms near Vineland, New Jersey. And they settled there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll go back to-- when I came to Stuttgart, Germany. I'd like to go back to that time of my life. In Stuttgart, Germany, I became a member of the Jewish Regional Committee. I have pictures to attest to it. I became very active. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I worked for the good of the Jewish people. I took part in a lot of demonstrations for the cause of Israel. I invented a commission to go to Israel to fight. I was freed because of my eyes. And, in 1950, I came to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brothers left Stuttgart in 1947. The youngest came 1946. And the two others left in 1947. And I came 1950, after-- the-- after the liquidation of the camp. I was the last one out. And my wife was the last one out because she was the director of the old shul there. And we had the pleasure of liquidating all of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the people at the camp were resettled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went-- they went to Israel, from Stuttgart to Israel. After the erection of the state of Israel, they went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So will you tell me, before, is that-- your kibbutz was-- was the kibbutz that was on the exodus?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the exodus. It was the leading kibbutz on the exodus, the real exodus, not the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. The picture was about the real exodus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's quite interesting to know that. So you came to the United States when, in 19--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States 1950-- February 1, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came. I couldn't do anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were a director and a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was everything. I was offered a job by the American Jewish Joint to go to Africa to connect with-- I didn't want to go. I traveled enough in my lifetime. I wanted to settle, to make a family, things like this. My wife was pregnant at the time with my son. So I decided to settle in the States and take it easy a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't take it easy a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little bit tired. And I joined the painters union. And I worked as a painter. In 1959, I was elected to a secretary-- it's a golden secretary of the Painters Local Union 1011, which was a Jewish [? chartered ?] local. And the minutes were written in Yiddish and English. I have a book which was given by the local to me as a present for remembrance. And I carried it till 1988. And-- and even so, after the merger, a number of locals in the ranks, I remained a secretary until 1984. I retired at the age of 80.
